1.description: (Color online) Bank Credit and Asset-to-Credit Ratio Notes. The left panel plots the median quarterly growth in bank credit (assets plus unused credit lines) of the 25 largest banks in each quarter from 2003Q1 to 2009Q2. The right panel plots the median asset-to-credit ratio.

2.description: (Color online) Impulse Responses Notes. The figure displays the impulse responses to a shift from the normal steady state to the crisis state that are discussed in Section 4.1 . It includes the dynamics of credit contraction Δ / ( Φ + 𝐿 ) Δ / ( Φ + L ) in the left panel and strategic drawdown in the right panel. Strategic drawdown is defined as the fraction of borrowers who draw down their credit lines despite not needing liquidity, which is represented as ( 𝐿 𝐷 − Λ ⁢ Φ ) / ( Φ − Λ ⁢ Φ ) ( L D − Λ ⁢ Φ ) / ( Φ − Λ ⁢ Φ ) . In addition to the responses in the estimated model (the solid lines), the figure depicts the impulse responses in two counterfactuals, one with a higher probability of substitution ( 𝜂 = 0 . 3 5 η = 0.35 , the dashed lines) and one with a higher rationing cost ( 𝛾 − = 0 . 1 3 γ − = 0.13 , the dash-dotted lines).

3.description: (Color online) Policy Counterfactuals Notes. The figure illustrates three sets of policy counterfactuals that are discussed in Section 5.2 . The first set of counterfactuals (dashed line with circles) implements CCFs by increasing the probability of substitution of borrowers after being separated from their credit lines ( x axis at the bottom). The second set of counterfactuals (dotted line with triangles) implements FFL schemes by reducing banks’ operating costs ( x axis at the top). The third set of counterfactuals (solid line with diamonds) implements both policies simultaneously. The vertical axis plots the differences in credit contraction in crises between the counterfactuals and the estimated model.
